1: Come, let us sing joyfully to the LORD; let us shout aloud to the Rock who saves us! — the opening imperative lekhu nerannenah (come, let us sing joyfully) is the call to communal worship that opens this and similar enthronement psalms. Hari'u (shout aloud) is the same battle-cry-as-worship word as Psalms 66:1 and Psalms 100:1 — the teru'ah (great shout) that served as both a military battle cry and the prescribed liturgical acclamation of the king's presence (Numbers 23:21; 1 Samuel 10:24; 2 Samuel 15:10). "The Rock who saves us" (tsur yish'enu) — the geological metaphor for divine protection and stability that runs throughout the Psalter and Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 32:4, 15, 18, 30-31).
2: Let us come before him with thanksgiving and celebrate him with songs of praise — the todah (thanksgiving) approach to the divine presence as the proper mode of entry into covenant worship. The imagery is processional: the community approaches (neqaddemah fanav, come before his face) with todah (thanksgiving offering/song) and zemirot (songs of praise). This processional approach corresponds to the festival pilgrimages to the temple — the three annual feasts when Israel came before YHWH's face in Zion (Exodus 23:15-17; Deuteronomy 16:16). The approach is not casual but structured by the covenant protocol of worship.
3: For the LORD is a great God, a great King greater than all other gods — the "greater than all other gods" formula does not necessarily assert that the other gods do not exist; it asserts YHWH's supremacy over all rival divine powers. This is the ANE henotheistic formula that the Psalter uses consistently (Psalms 96:4; Psalms 97:7, 9; Psalms 135:5; Psalms 136:2). In the context of the Babylonian exile (the implied setting of Book IV), where the apparent defeat of Israel might suggest the defeat of YHWH, the assertion of his supremacy over all divine powers is a polemical claim. Paul uses similar language in 1 Corinthians 8:5-6 ("even if there are so-called gods... for us there is but one God, the Father").
4: The deepest places of the earth are in his hand, and the mountain peaks belong to him — the vertical axis of creation from its deepest recesses to its highest points is entirely under divine sovereignty. The "depths of the earth" (mechqere aretz) refers to the subterranean realm — including the foundations of the world and the realm of Sheol (Psalms 63:9; Psalms 71:20). The mountain peaks (taphot harim) are the highest accessible points in creation. The vertical totality claim is a creation-theology statement: every point in the created order is within YHWH's hand, not merely the pleasant middle zone of human habitation.
5: The sea is his — he made it; his own hands shaped the dry land — the sea and dry land are the two great regions distinguished on the third day of creation (Genesis 1:9-10). YHWH's ownership of the sea is grounded in his making it: the creator has sovereign authority over what he creates. This is an implicit Baal polemic: Baal was the storm god who supposedly controlled the sea and defeated Yam, but Ps 95 asserts that the sea itself belongs to YHWH because he made it. The land shaped by divine hands (yabbashot yadav yatzerahu) is the same language as the pottery-metaphor of human creation (Genesis 2:7; Psalms 139:13-15) applied to the entire terrestrial surface.
6: Come, let us bow down and worship; let us kneel before the LORD who made us — the three verbs of covenant submission: bowing down (nishtachaveh, prostration — the posture of vassals before a suzerain), worshipping (nikera'h, bowing/kneeling), and kneeling (nivrekha, bending the knee). The physical postures of the body enact the relational reality of covenant submission. "Who made us" (oshenu) — the creation-relationship as the basis for covenant obligation: YHWH's authority over us is grounded not only in covenant history but in the prior fact of creation. The one who made the earth (v5) has also made the worshipping community.
7: For he is our God, and we are the people of his pasture, the flock he tends — the shepherd-flock image that runs from the Psalter's most personal psalm (Ps 23: "YHWH is my shepherd") through the communal confession here to the prophetic vision of Ezek 34 (YHWH as the good shepherd who will rescue his flock from bad shepherds) and to Jesus' explicit application of Ezek 34 to himself (John 10:11-18). The "pasture" (mar'itu) is the covenantal provision — the land, the guidance, the feeding — that YHWH provides for the community that belongs to him. "Today — if only you would hear his voice" — the urgent present tense that pivots the psalm from praise to warning.
8: 'Do not make your hearts stubborn, as at Meribah — as on that day at Massah in the wilderness' — the historical warning embedded in the call to worship. Meribah ("quarreling") and Massah ("testing") are the names given to the place where Israel tested YHWH by demanding water in the wilderness (Exodus 17:1-7). Two separate incidents are associated with these names: Exod 17 at the beginning of the wilderness wandering and Numbers 20:2-13 at the end, where Moses himself sinned. Deuteronomy 6:16 explicitly commands: "Do not test YHWH your God as you tested him at Massah." The psalm invites the community into worship and immediately confronts them with their ancestors' failure to trust.
9: 'Your ancestors put me to the test and challenged me, even after seeing all I had done' — the specification of the wilderness generation's sin: testing YHWH despite having seen the evidence. This is the most severe form of unbelief in the Psalter and the Deuteronomic history: not the unbelief of those who have no evidence but the unbelief of those who have overwhelming evidence and choose to act as if they had none. The Exodus generation witnessed the ten plagues, the Reed Sea crossing, the daily manna, and the water from the rock — and still demanded tests of YHWH's presence and power. Hebrews 3:7-19 applies this pattern directly to the Christian community as a warning against the same hardness of heart.
10: 'For forty years I was provoked by that generation and declared, "This people's heart keeps wandering; they have never learned my ways"' — the forty-year wilderness period as the duration of divine provocation. The fundamental accusation is not specific behavioral failures but a continuous failure of orientation: the heart that "keeps wandering" (to'im lev hem) — the to'eh (wandering, going astray) is the pastoral image of the sheep that goes its own way rather than following the shepherd (Isaiah 53:6; Psalms 119:176). "They have never learned my ways" — not ignorance of the facts of YHWH's acts (they had seen everything) but the failure to internalize those facts as a shaping knowledge of who YHWH is and how to live with him.
11: 'So in my anger I swore: "They will never enter my rest"' — the divine oath of exclusion that closes the psalm with the most sobering possible note. The "rest" (menuhatiy) is the covenant rest that YHWH had prepared for his people — first the land of Canaan (Deuteronomy 3:20; Psalms 12:9-10; Joshua 21:44) and, in the hermeneutics of Hebrews, the eschatological rest of which the land was a foretaste (Hebrews 3:7-4:11). Hebrews 3-4 treats Psalms 95:11 as the key text for understanding the present urgency of the gospel: if the wilderness generation forfeited the rest through unbelief, the Christian community must heed the same "today" (Psalms 95:7) before the same oath of exclusion is pronounced over them.
